file.content:
# Review: deepmind/429.lean

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line): `import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`.
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line.
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# ErdÅ‘s Problem 429`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link to `erdosproblems.com/429`.
- [ ] **Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem.** The module docstring contains only the heading, reference link, and a bibliography entry `[We24]`. The actual problem description is entirely in the theorem docstring instead. A brief summary should be added to the module docstring (e.g., "ErdÅ‘s asked whether every infinite admissible set of natural numbers has a translate contained in the primes. Weisenberg showed the answer is no.").
- [x] Optional bibliography entries: `[We24]` citation is present.

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos429 ... end Erdos429`.
- [x] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase`: `Erdos429`.
- [x] No `open`/`open scoped` statements needed; none present. N/A.

## 5. Custom Definitions

- N/A â€” no custom `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations in this file.

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] Uses the `theorem` keyword.
- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_429` (snake_case, matching namespace number).
- [x] Name follows Lean/Mathlib `snake_case` convention.
- [x] Has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research solved, AMS 11]`.
- [x] Theorem has a docstring (`/-- ... -/`).
- [x] Docstring references the source paper: `[We24]`.

### `answer()` usage

The original problem asks a yes/no question ("Is it true that..."), so **Case 1** applies.

- [x] Theorem type is `answer(False) â†” P`, matching Case 1 format.
- [x] Since the problem has been solved in the negative (Weisenberg's counterexample), `answer(False)` is correct.
- [x] The RHS `P` formalizes the question as originally asked (the affirmative claim: every infinite admissible set has a prime translate), so `answer(True)` would mean "yes" and `answer(False)` means "no".
- [x] Quantifiers (`âˆ€ (A : Set â„•), ...`) appear **after** `answer(False)` on the RHS of `â†”`, not as theorem parameters.

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[category ...]` attribute present.
- [x] Value is `research solved` â€” valid category.
- [x] Not marked `research open`, so the `sorry`-free constraint does not apply.

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute present.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed: `11`.
- [x] Numbers in ascending order (single entry).
- [x] No duplicates.
- [x] `11` is a valid AMS MSC2020 code (Number Theory).

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Uses `by` with `sorry` on the following line (multi-line form, acceptable per checklist).
- [x] No `sorry` in definitions (no definitions present).
- [x] No informal proof sketches for open research problems (this is `research solved`).
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- N/A â€” no variants present.

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used: `âˆ€`, `âˆƒ`, `âˆˆ`, `â†”`, `â„•`, `â‰ `.
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all under 100 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] Not verified in this review (requires `lake build`).

---

## Summary

**1 issue found:**

1. **Module docstring missing plain-English description** (Â§3): The module docstring has the heading, reference link, and bibliography, but no description of the problem. The full problem description lives only in the theorem docstring. A brief summary should be added to the module docstring.

Everything else conforms to the checklist. The `answer(False) â†” P` usage is correct for a yes/no question answered in the negative, the formalization of admissibility (`âˆƒ r : ZMod p, âˆ€ a âˆˆ A, (a : ZMod p) â‰  r`) is faithful to the mathematical definition, and all style/attribute requirements are satisfied.

file.content:
/-
Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.

Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
You may obtain a copy of the License at

    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
limitations under the License.
-/

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# ErdÅ‘s Problem 429

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/429](https://www.erdosproblems.com/429)

[We24] Weisenberg, *A counterexample to ErdÅ‘s' conjecture on sparse sets and primes*, 2024.
-/

namespace Erdos429

/--
Is it true that, if $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is sparse enough and does not cover all residue
classes modulo $p$ for any prime $p$, then there exists some $n$ such that $n + a$
is prime for all $a \in A$?

A set $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is called "admissible" if for every prime $p$, there exists a
residue class modulo $p$ not represented in $A$. This is a necessary condition
for the translate $A + n$ to consist entirely of primes.

Weisenberg [We24] has shown the answer is no: $A$ can be arbitrarily sparse
and missing at least one residue class modulo every prime $p$, and yet $A + n$
is not contained in the primes for any $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

We formalize the conjecture for infinite admissible sets $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$.
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 11]
theorem erdos_429 :
    answer(False) â†”
    âˆ€ (A : Set â„•),
      Set.Infinite A â†’
      (âˆ€ p : â„•, p.Prime â†’ âˆƒ r : ZMod p, âˆ€ a âˆˆ A, (a : ZMod p) â‰  r) â†’
      âˆƒ n : â„•, âˆ€ a âˆˆ A, (n + a).Prime := by
  sorry

end Erdos429

# ErdÅ‘s Problem 429

ErdÅ‘s asked whether every infinite admissible set of natural numbers has a translate contained
in the primes. Weisenberg showed the answer is no.

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/429](https://www.erdosproblems.com/429)

[We24] Weisenberg, *A counterexample to ErdÅ‘s' conjecture on sparse sets and primes*, 2024.
-/

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# ErdÅ‘s Problem 429

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/429](https://www.erdosproblems.com/429)

[We24] Weisenberg, *A counterexample to ErdÅ‘s' conjecture on sparse sets and primes*, 2024.
-/

namespace Erdos429

/--
Is it true that, if $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is sparse enough and does not cover all residue
classes modulo $p$ for any prime $p$, then there exists some $n$ such that $n + a$
is prime for all $a \in A$?

A set $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is called "admissible" if for every prime $p$, there exists a
residue class modulo $p$ not represented in $A$. This is a necessary condition
for the translate $A + n$ to consist entirely of primes.

Weisenberg [We24] has shown the answer is no: $A$ can be arbitrarily sparse
and missing at least one residue class modulo every prime $p$, and yet $A + n$
is not contained in the primes for any $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

We formalize the conjecture for infinite admissible sets $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$.
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 11]
theorem erdos_429 :
    answer(False) â†”
    âˆ€ (A : Set â„•),
      Set.Infinite A â†’
      (âˆ€ p : â„•, p.Prime â†’ âˆƒ r : ZMod p, âˆ€ a âˆˆ A, (a : ZMod p) â‰  r) â†’
      âˆƒ n : â„•, âˆ€ a âˆˆ A, (n + a).Prime := by
  sorry

end Erdos429
